narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a solid middle-income family background, you've put real effort into your law school journey, including your undergraduate studies and LSAT preparation, while planning to attend full-time. The admissions model we use predicted a 68.9% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which unfortunately led to the rejection of your application—our goal is to select students with the strongest likelihood of bar success to ensure everyone thrives. This prediction was driven primarily by your academic decile rankings from school, particularly your Year 3 performance, which fell in the lower range compared to the typical student's mid-level standing, strongly pushing the model toward expecting a bar failure. Your Year 1 decile was similarly influential and below average, reinforcing that concern as the second-biggest factor in the decision. On a more positive note, your undergraduate GPA of 3.00, while slightly below the average of 3.21, worked in your favor by nudging the prediction toward bar passage. Your LSAT score of 32, lower than the typical 36.7, had a minor positive pull too, but it wasn't enough to offset the top concerns. Your middle family income bracket and full-time enrollment plans each played smaller roles in tipping toward the failure prediction, as they align with patterns seen in some struggling bar takers. Compared to typical admitted students, your grades placed you in the lower deciles while your test scores lagged behind the stronger profiles that predict bar success. I know this is disappointing, especially given your dedication, but it's not a reflection of your potential—it's just one data-driven snapshot. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your later-year grades through additional coursework or post-bac programs, and gathering letters highlighting your resilience and growth. Many students in similar positions have improved these key areas and succeeded on later applications. We're rooting for you to build on your strengths and reapply stronger. Keep pushing—you've got this.
